coredata.dc:title: Nonlinear optical switching behavior of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiao
Bai, Liang
Shen, Lei
Yao, Bowen
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Song, Yinglin
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging class of carbon materials, have been recently demonstrated to possess nonlinear optical (NLO) behavior (e.g. nonlinear saturable absorption and reverse saturable absorption) which potentially expands CDs new application in optical limiting field. However, NLO switching behavior of CDs has not yet been reported in previous literature. In this paper, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) prepared from 2,3-diaminophenazine showed a switchable NLO behavior from saturable absorption (SA) to reverse saturable absorption (RSA) when the laser energy of incident picosecond pulse increased. By contrast, upon the irradiation of nanosecond pulse, the N-CDs exhibited only RSA behavior through excited state absorption (ESA) process. In mechanism, the switching behavior was probably resulted from competition between ground-state absorption and free carrier absorption (FCA) or two phonon absorption (TPA) processes, where both nitrogen doping and carbonyl groups of N-CDs were proved to be indispensable for the switching behavior.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N−CDs
Z−scan
Optical switching behaviour

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical abstract of nonlinear optical switching behavior of N-CDs.
Open aperture Z-scan curves of N-CDs aqueous solution upon irradiation of 532 nm laser at different input energy with duration time of 21 ps. Solid lines represent theoretical fits.
Schematic energy level diagram of N-CDs.
Open aperture Z-scan curves of N-CDs aqueous solution at different input energy under 532 nm laser excitation with duration time of 4 ns. Solid lines represent theoretical fits.
NLA coefficient β of N-CDs versus input intensities of picosecond and nanosecond pulses.
Open aperture Z-scan curves of CD-1 aqueous solution at different input energy under 532 nm laser excitation with duration time of 21 ps.

head.ce:title: Nonlinear optical switching behavior of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Soochow University, Suzhou, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Soochow University, Suzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging class of carbon materials, have been recently demonstrated to possess nonlinear optical (NLO) behavior (e.g. nonlinear saturable absorption and reverse saturable absorption) which potentially expands CDs new application in optical limiting field. However, NLO switching behavior of CDs has not yet been reported in previous literature. In this paper, nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) prepared from 2,3-diaminophenazine showed a switchable NLO behavior from saturable absorption (SA) to reverse saturable absorption (RSA) when the laser energy of incident picosecond pulse increased. By contrast, upon the irradiation of nanosecond pulse, the N-CDs exhibited only RSA behavior through excited state absorption (ESA) process. In mechanism, the switching behavior was probably resulted from competition between ground-state absorption and free carrier absorption (FCA) or two phonon absorption (TPA) processes, where both nitrogen doping and carbonyl groups of N-CDs were proved to be indispensable for the switching behavior.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as one class of carbon nanoparticles defined by ultrafine size below 10 nm, have showed tunable optical properties (e.g. photoluminescence and electroluminescence) and promising applications in display and diagnosis [1–7]. Recently, CDs nonlinear optical (NLO) properties have also been discovered, significantly expanding their potential applications in optoelectronic and photonic devices. Accoding to the previous liteature, the CDs NLO behavior was determined by their size and surface functional groups [8]. For example, the third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ (3) of CDs depended on the size and amount of carboxyl groups [8]. However, up to now, NLO switching behavior has not been reported for CDs [9–11].

Chemical doping is one of powerful tools to improve CDs optical performance through tuning their electronic structures. For example, boron, nitrogen and phosphorous doping have been reported to tune the NLO optical response of CDs [12–17]. Herein, N-CDs was synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminophenazine and urea, and showed a RSA behavior contributing from the excited state absorption (ESA) under the laser irradiation of nanosecond pulse. Interestingly, upon the laser irradition of picosecond pulse, NLO switching behavior was observed when laser energy increased. In detail, with the increment of incident laser energy (wavelength: 532 nm), N-CDs transformed from nonlinear saturable absorption (SA) behavior to reverse saturable absorption (RSA) behavior due to free carrier absorption (FCA) and/or two photon absorption (TPA) process (Fig. 1 ). By contrast, the switching behavior was not observed for CDs after either removal of nitrogen source in the preparation or elimination of carbonyl group through chemical aftertreatment with NaBH4, indicating both of the nitrogen doping and carbonyl group might account for the NLO transformation. In final, N-CDs can be a good candidate for optical limiting (OL) materials due to its advantage of absorbing of excessive laser energy without a compromise of linear transmittance.

Synthesis of N-CDs. The N-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method. Firstly, 5 mL of 0.01 mg mL−1 2,3-diaminophenazine containing 0.5 mg of urea was ultrasonically treated for 20 min, followed by hydrothermal reaction in Hastelloy autoclave at 200 °C for 24 h. Then, the resultant light brown supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water for 4 days with a semipermeable membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 500–1000 Da) to remove the precursor and small molecules.

Preparation of CDs-1. 10 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaBH4 solution was added to 5 mL of N-CDs solution, followed by agitation for 12 h at room temperature. RN-CDs was then obtained after dialyzing the resultant solution.
Preparation of CDs-2. 5 mL of 0.01 mg mL−1 citric acid was added by 0.5 mg of urea. Following a procedure the same to that of synthesizing N-CDs, CDs-2 was obtained.

Preparation of CDs-3. 5 mL of 0.01 mg mL−1 2,3-diaminophenazine solution was directly ultrasonically treated for 20 min, Following a procedure the same to that of synthesizing N-CDs, CDs-3 was then obtained.

Preparation of CDs-4. CDs-4 was prepared through electrochemical oxidation method in two-electrode system, where two graphite rods as working and counter electrodes were placed parallelly (with a separation of 7.5 cm), and were applied a constant voltage of 30 V for 2 weeks in a 1.84 mol L−1 sulfuric acid electrolyte. CDs-4 was then obtained after the resultant electrolyte was dialyzed against deionized water for 4 days with a semipermeable membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 500–1000 Da).

Characterizations. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained by a transmission electron microscope with the accelerating voltage of 200 kV (FEI Corporation, American). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, American). Raman spectra were collected on an HR 800 Raman spectroscope equipped with a synapse CCD detector and a confocal Olympus microscope (HORIBA, France). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained with a Bruker Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Hyperion, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured by an ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Kratos, England).

Z-scan Measurements. The Z-scan measurements were conducted by using two different laser devices (Fig. S3): an adjustable Q-switched Nd: YAG laser (Continuum Surelite II, USA) with a pulse time of 4 ns; a mode-locked Nd: YAG laser (EKSPLA, LTU) with a pulse time of 21 ps (Both them performed at excitation wavelength of 532 nm with repetition rate of 10 Hz). In detail, the sample was moved along the direction of the incident laser, and then the energy of laser passing through the aperture behind the sample were recorded against the longitudinal coordinate z of samples. The incident laser with different energy were also employed to study the NLO behaviors (5.2–10 μj for nanosecond Z-scan measurement and 2–13 μj for picosecond Z-scan measurement).

The N-CDs were facily prepared via hytrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminophenazine and urea. The contrast experiments have been done to prove N-CDs own the property of NLO switching behavior. CD-2 (citric acid and urea) and CD-3 (2,3-diaminophenazine) were done in the same condition fabrication of N-CDs. In addition, CD-4 (sulfur doped carbon dots) was fabricated from electrochemical corrosion. The detailed morphologies and chemical structures of N-CDs were studied by TEM, UV–Vis and Raman spectra. N-CDs comprised a conjuagated graphitic core with amorphouous surface, having an uniform size with an average diameter of 2–5 nm (Figs. S1a–b) [18]. The lattice spacing of graphitic core was measured to be 0.21 nm, correspoinding to the ⟨100⟩ lattice planes of graphitic carbon (the inserted picture in Fig. S1a). In UV–Vis spectrum, N-CDs showed a clear UV band at about 270 nm and a shoulder peak at round 325 nm, ascribing to π–π* and n–π* transitions (Fig. S1c) [19,20]. Raman spectrum (Fig. S1d) is a powerful tool to study the defect degree of carbon materials. N-CDs showed two bands: a G band at ~1589 cm−1, associated with graphite domains, and a D band at 1370 cm−1, related with structural defects at N-CDs surfaces. The intensities ratio of these two bands (ID/IG) can be used to quantify the defect degree, which was calculated to be 0.95 for the obtained N-CDs.

The chemical compositions and structures of N-CDs were studied by XPS and IR spectra. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. S2a) clearly reveals that N-CDs have a high nitrogen content of 36.36% [21,22]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S2b) can be grouped into four various peaks: C–C (284.5 eV), C–N–C (285.5 eV), C–O/C–NH2 (287.2 eV) and CO (288.3 eV) [23,24]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) revealed the existence of graphite nitrogen (398.5 eV) and amino groups (400.5 eV) [25]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S2d) can be divided into three peaks: O–H (529.7 eV), C–O (530.7 eV) and CO (531.9 eV) [24]. FTIR spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. S2e) revealed the existence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups, displaying several absorption peaks including O–H (3435.5 cm−1), N–H (3252.3 cm−1), C–H (2923.9 cm−1), N–H (800 cm−1), CC (1568 cm−1), CO (1630.8 cm−1), N−(C)3 (1349 cm−1), C–N–C (1267 cm−1), and C–O (1050 cm−1). (Fig. S2f).

The conventional Z-scan measurements were conducted to analyze the NLO properties of N-CDs. The profile shape (Fig. S3) of open aperture Z-scan upon the irradiation of picosecond pulses closely related with the input energy of laser. At a low input energy (e.g. 2.0 uj), the normalized transmission curve (Fig. 2 a) showed a symmetrical peak with the center at Z = 0 mm (the position of focus of laser), corresponding to the SA process caused by electron transition between ground state and the first excited state. Interestingly, when input energy increased to 5.1 uj (Fig. 2b), a symmetrical weak transmission valley appeared at around the focus point, and then turned stronger with the increment of input energy (Fig. 2c), indicating the more contributions of RSA resulted from electron transition between two excited state energy levels of N-CDs. Finally, when the input energy increased to 13 uj, N-CDs showed a complete RSA behavior (Fig. 2d).

The NLO switch mechanism can be explained as follows (Fig. 3 ): (i) at the low input energy, N-CDs showed a SA behavior due to ground state bleaching; (ii) upon the increment of laser energy, no more ground electron could be excited to the first excited state, which thus impaired SA performance of N-CDs; (iii) when the energy of laser was further enhanced, electrons in the excited states could transit to higher ones through the process of free carrier absorption (FCA) and two photon absorption (TPA), leading to RSA phenomenon [26,27].

In addition, the NLO properties of N-CDs upon laser irradiation of nanosecond pulses was also studied (Fig. 4 ). N-CDs exhibit a transmission valley and it becomes deeper with the input energy increased from 5.2 to 10 μj, resulting in the stronger RSA of N-CDs.

As for open aperture Z-scan, the normalized transmittance can be expressed as the following equations:

where the I0, Leff, z and z0 represent on-axis peak intensity at focus, effective interaction length, longitudinal displacement of sample from focus and Rayleigh diffraction length, respectively.
α
( I )
is total absorption coefficient at the laser energy of I, which can be expressed as follows:
(2)
α
( I )
=

S
+ βI
where the
α
0
, IS and
β
represent linear absorption coefficient (124.3 m−1 at 532 nm), saturation intensity coefficient and the effective third-order nonlinear absorption coefficient.

On the basis of the above model, the NLA results can be well theoretically fitted by using the coefficient Is = 7 × 1010 W cm−2. With the increment of input intensity, β kept almost constant at 4 × 10−11 cm W−1 when N-CDs were irradiated by picosecond pulses (Fig. 5 a). However, in the cases that nanosecond pulses was used, β increased from (Fig. 5b) as the input light intensity increased from 5.2 to 10 uj, indicating the ESA behavior became more dominated.

The structure-effect relationships of CDs NLO properties were also studied [8,26]. CDs with different chemical structures were prepared, including nitrogen doped CDs without carbonyl group (CDs-1), the detailed characterization can see Figs. S4–S8; nitrogen-doped CDs prepared from citric acid (CDs-2), non-nitrogen-doped CDs (CDs-3), sulfur-doped CDs (CDs-4) and more detailed characterization can be seen from Figs. S9–S11. The open aperture picosecond Z-scan (Fig. 6 , Fig. S11) also showed no NLA switching behavior for all these CDs as the input energy increased from 5 to 13 μj.

In here, N-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminophenazine and urea, and showed an abnormal switch from SA to RSA properties when the laser energy of incident picosecond pulses increased. Control experiments demonstrated that both of the nitrogen doping and carbonyl groups were responsible for optical switching behavior. In final, the NLO switching behavior endows N-CDs a better candidate to the applications of mode lock lasers and optical limiter.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.
